author: @AndyMuellerez2b
text: I recently retired, and I'm working harder than ever since then, especially with learning.  I have especially enjoyed your videos on casein - very easy to follow and informative.  The big question I have now is which types of plastic can be mixed together and which types should never be mixed together?  I have a feeling that if I watch more of your videos, I'll find out.  Thanks for making these.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: My pleasure amte - and i will be doing more on these plastics in the future

author: @MrJmto1830
text: I have a few questions. First is thermal bio plastic safe to eat, for instance if my dog gets a hold of it and eats it, is it harmful? And the other question would be, can a liquid form be manufactured in aerosol cans as a spray like the rubber spray (flex seal)?if so what would you recommend?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: The plastic won't poison him in itself but there are quite a lot of additives in plastics and it would depend on the manufacturer - so not a good idea. It would also be fairly indigestible in itself - it does biodegrade but the action is slow - a lot slower than it would take to go through the digestive tract. Eating plastic can therefore lead to intestinal torsion - you might want to see a vet. The second answer is sure - there are lots of ways to approach this problem but you would have to be more specific - for example polyurethane spray paints would be an example of a successful application that fulfils your question but I suspect what you are asking is more complex than that

author: @thagrit
text: Hey Robert I don't have an academic paper question for you. This question arises from your previous videos. Specifically the video about graphitic carbon nitride. Thanks for sharing how to make it from urea by the way. You said that in the presence of sunlight and co2 it catalyzes co2 into ethanol and methanol unless I am mistaken.  The question that arises here is can you concentrate atmospheric carbon dioxide to feed this process? I suppose you could do the vinegar and baking soda for co2 but it would be much nicer to simply take it out of the air. Unless of course that process is to expensive or difficult to do.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hiya mate - no it is more than possible to do what you suggest and is part of my artificial leaf research

author: @bigwoofbouncer
text:
Hi Robert, 
Firstly, awesome channel, I'm only just starting to watch your videos and loving every one! My question is this: are there possible applications for your graphene ink, in the production of cheap super-capacitors for use in large scale electrical grid load balancing or for longer-term storage of excess electricity generated by wind and solar? I'm by no means an expert, just curious about it, as I feel new base-load and balancing technologies are what will make renewable power generation a viable replacement for fossil fuels. My apologies if you've already covered this elsewhere and I just haven't seen it yet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Glad you like the channel mate and yes i think the ins would be good for supercaps particularly using print methods

author: @casualjoe2
text:
Hi Rob, I really like your inks, I've played with them a bit and they are very interesting, cheers for making them, your videos have been consistently awesome too.

You could probably guess that I am making a supercapacitor based bicycle braking energy regen system out of junk like old toroidal inductors and I want to tell everyone how to build it once it's done, just need to get a new battery for me camera.

My question, and one of the reasons I bought your waterproof ink, is to ask whether it is possible to build some carbon/nickle/nickle oxide electrodes to split water at voltages as low as 1.5V, like in that amazing paper form Stanford a few months ago..

http://www.nature.com/ncomms/2014/140822/ncomms5695/full/ncomms5695.html 

The method I tried goes something like:   
Paint small plastic sheets with ink, add current collectors, dry out in the oven
Plate with Nickle untill it looks about 75% covered
Turn off the power supply and wait for the freshly plated Nickle to corrode a little bit 
Remove the plated sheet and try to use 2 of them to electrolyse water.

Tried this with Tin as well.

It is a bit of a simpleton method and it worked like shit (tiny bubbles at 10V) but thought I'd just put that out there as a starting point haha.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very interesting mate - can I suggest you try putting a bit of urea into your water and see what happens - say about 0.33 molar

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you have to get that camer up mate - i'd love to see some of what you are doing

author: @solarhope
text:
Hi Robert,
I have been enjoying your channel for the past year or so. I have enjoyed your enthusiasm, depth of knowledge and your northern straight talking approach.
I realize that the direction of some of your work is already influenced via the excellent comments you receive on your vids, however inviting direct non related questions is a great idea.
To start the ball rolling may I suggest a DIY flow battery using relatively safe and obtainable chemicals, I really think this could be a game changer in the alternative energy field.
Regards Martin.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have been thinking about flow batteries already to be honest - so no worries lol

replies.author: @solarhope
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
That's great Robert, I shall look forward to that, and if you raise money from donation or some other means I am happy to go with the flow - no pun intended :-)   Martin.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

solarhope
